entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: AN APOSTLE.
entries.body: Paul begins this epistle differently, because his subject matter is different. He writes that he is an apostle by the will of God, alluding to those false apostles who had not been sent by Christ and whose teaching was not true. There were many sects which had emerged and which preached Christ according to their own whims. They broke up churches, and some of their dried-up branches are still with us today. For this reason, Paul sets out everything which is opposed to the heresies and asserts that he is a true preacher because he has been sent by Christ, according to God’s will. {fn:0}

footnotes.text: CSEL 81.3:4. For an explanation of the omission here of book, section and subsection numbering in line-by-line commentaries, see the introduction (p. xxii).

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: CALLED BY GOD’S WILL.
entries.body: From the very beginning Paul casts down the Corinthians’ pride, in that he speaks of himself as called. For what I have learned, says Paul, I did not discover myself, but it was while I was persecuting the church that I was called. It was God who willed that you too should be saved in this way. We have done nothing good by ourselves, but by God’s will we have been saved. We were called because it seemed good to him, not because we were worthy. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 1.1

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: OUR BROTHER.
entries.body: By calling Sosthenes his brother, Paul is both demonstrating his own humility and pointing out that Sosthenes is a fellow worker in the gospel. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 1

footnotes.text: PL 30:717B. For an explanation of the inclusion here of comments attributed to the archheretic Pelagius, see the introduction (p. xxii).

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: NOT CALLED BY MAN.
entries.body: Paul is saying in effect: You Corinthians have been called by men, but I have been called by God. I think that Sosthenes was a Corinthian. He is mentioned in Acts [18:17], where Luke says that in the time of Gallio the Greeks arrested him and beat him. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 165

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: SANCTIFIED IN CHRIST.
entries.body: Why did Paul write to those called to be saints as well as to those who are already sanctified and in the church? Surely this means that the letter is addressed not only to those who are already cleansed from their sins but also to those who still await cleansing, though they are among those whom God has called. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE CHURCH AS A WHOLE.
entries.body:
Paul writes to the church as a whole, because at that time leaders had not yet been appointed for individual churches. He censures them for many things, but in spite of that he still says that they have been sanctified. However, they later began to behave badly, so that although the whole church was sanctified in Christ, some members of it had been deflected from the truth by the wicked teaching of the false apostles.

The Corinthians were called to be saints, which means that they could not deviate from the narrow path of sanctification. Paul linked them, as Gentiles, with the true Jews, because salvation is of the Jews, so that wherever there are Gentiles who call on the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and wherever there are true Jews, both are united in him. But the false apostles, who preached the name of Christ in accordance with the wisdom of this world, criticized the law and the prophets. Like Marcion and Mani, they maintained that Christ was not really crucified but that it merely appeared that he had been. Neither did they believe in the resurrection of the body. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: THE LETTER WRITTEN TO THE SAINTS.
entries.body: Paul is writing to those who have preserved their sanctity, not to those who have lost it. The former he honors with his letter; the latter he admonishes with his authority. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 1

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE ONE ASSEMBLY ALL OVER THE WORLD.
entries.body: The church ought to be united because it belongs to God. It does not exist only in Corinth, but all over the world, and it is one, for the church’s name (ekklēsia) means assembly. It is not a name of separation, but a name of unity and concord. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 1.1

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: TO HEAL DIVISIONS.
entries.body: Everything Paul mentions here is meant to be a remedy for the disease of schism. They are meant to heal division and display the church’s unity. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 165

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: GRACE FROM THE FATHER.
entries.body: Paul teaches that Christ should be invoked in prayer but all grace comes from the Father. The two are one in their divinity, but primacy belongs to the authority of the Father. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: GRACE AND PEACE.
entries.body: If our peace comes from God’s grace, why are you so proud, since you are saved by grace? How can anyone find grace with God, except through humility? {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 1.3

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: FATHER AND SON ARE ONE.
entries.body: Paul says that Christ is their benefactor as well as the Father, demonstrating that the two are one. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 166

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: ALL THE TIME.
entries.body: Paul does not just give thanks for the Corinthians some of the time, nor only when they do good. He puts himself in the position of a father, who is thankful for his children all the time, whatever they may have done. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: BY FAITH ALONE.
entries.body: God has decreed that a person who believes in Christ can be saved without works. By faith alone he receives the forgiveness of his sins. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: NOT BY WORKS.
entries.body: You were saved by grace, not by works. And who gave you this grace? It was not Paul, or another apostle, but Jesus Christ himself. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 2.3

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: A SWEET BEGINNING.
entries.body: Paul takes care to sweeten their ears before starting to admonish them. What he says is true, however: He gives thanks to God because of the gifts which have been given to them. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 166

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: SPEECH GOES FURTHER.
entries.body: Knowledge shows what there is to know. Speech goes further and explains it. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: STEADFAST IN GRACE TO ACQUIRE KNOWLEDGE.
entries.body: This means that the Corinthians have remained steadfast in the grace which they have received and in the preaching of the doctrine of truth, because they have acquired spiritual knowledge. Paul therefore gives thanks to God for these things. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: ONCE BLESSED WITH GRACE.
entries.body: When the Corinthians came to faith in Christ they were blessed with all grace. But now that they were no longer living according to their faith, they had made void the grace which they had received. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 1

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: TO UNDERSTAND AND TO SPEAK.
entries.body: There is knowledge which goes beyond speech. Many have knowledge which they cannot express—for example, those who are uneducated and unable to express themselves clearly. But the Corinthians were not like them. They were able both to understand and to speak. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 2.3

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: CONFIRMED IN US.
entries.body: The testimony of Christ is confirmed in us if we can say, like the apostle Paul, I am persuaded that neither life nor death etc. can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord [Rom 8:38-39]. But if we are upset by every little thing that happens, then Christ’s testimony has not been confirmed in us at all. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE TESTIMONY STRENGTHENED BY FAITH.
entries.body: The testimony of Christ has been confirmed in them because they have been strengthened by their faith. They had come to put no trust in human things. Rather, all their hope was in Christ, for they were ensnared neither by pleasure nor by the enticements of pleasure. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: PRAISE BEFORE CRITICISM.
entries.body: These praises are not uncritical, as the rest of the epistle makes clear. But they are inserted in order to prepare the Corinthians for the criticism which is to come. For whoever starts out with unpleasant words antagonizes his hearers. Paul starts by praising them in order to avoid this. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 2.5

entries.author: St. Jerome
entries.title: UNIMPEACHABLE IN THE PRESENT.
entries.body: Although we lack no gift, nevertheless we await the appearance of our Lord Jesus Christ. He will then keep us secure in all things and present us unimpeachable when the day of our Lord Jesus Christ comes. The end of the world shall arrive, when no flesh may glory in his sight. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: WAITING FOR THE REVEALING.
entries.body: In this life the righteous person does not yet enjoy what he hopes for but rather endures suffering and danger. He is waiting for the revelation of Christ to come. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: BELIEVERS AND UNBELIEVERS AWAIT THAT DAY.
entries.body: It is clear that Paul was a circumspect man who was full of concern as he awaited the day of judgment. On that day the Lord Jesus Christ will be revealed both to believers and to unbelievers. Then unbelievers will realize that what they did not want to believe is in fact true. Believers will rejoice, finding that what they believed in is more wonderful than they had imagined. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: THE REVELATION IS NOW HIDDEN.
entries.body: Christ’s second coming is described as a revelation, because now it is hidden from us. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 1

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: SUSTAINED FOREVER.
entries.body: Who sustains us? Christ Jesus, the Word and Wisdom of God. Moreover, he sustains us not merely for a day or two, but forever. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: GUILTLESS IN THE DAY OF OUR LORD.
entries.body: Paul is confident that the Corinthians will persevere in righteousness until the day of judgment. People who could not be shaken in spite of so many turmoils and disagreements proved that they would remain faithful to the end. In praising them, Paul is also challenging those who had been corrupted by the errors of the false apostles, for in proclaiming the faith of the former, he is calling the latter to repentance. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: HIDDEN REPROOF.
entries.body: This is not praise but backhanded reproof, since the Corinthians were far from guiltless, as the rest of the epistle makes clear. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 2.7

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: IF NOW GUILTY, THEN GUILTLESS.
entries.body: By saying that he hopes that they will be guiltless in the day of Christ Jesus, Paul is indicating that right now they are guilty. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 167

entries.author: St. Clement of Alexandria
entries.title: THE GOD WHO IS FAITHFUL.
entries.body: That God is faithful means that we can trust his self-revelation. His Word reveals him. He is the God who is faithful. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE PROBLEM IS NOT WITH GOD.
entries.body: Paul says this so that the Corinthians will not fall into despair when he criticizes them. He reminds them that God is not the problem. We are, because of our sin and unbelief. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 2.7

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: CALLED TO BE ONE WITH CHRIST.
entries.body: Believe in Christ always, because you were called for no other purpose than to be one with us in him. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: BE FAITHFUL TO YOUR ADOPTION.
entries.body: Fellowship is brotherhood. Just as Paul declares God’s unfailing faithfulness toward us in this regard, so we ourselves must not be found to be faithless or dishonorable with respect to our adoption. Rather we must remain faithful in it. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: LET THERE BE NO DISSENSIONS.
entries.body: The visible church is a mixed body, consisting of both righteous and unrighteous people. This is why Paul praises some of its members and criticizes others. The person who agrees with the right doctrine and the church’s teaching concerning the Father, Son and Holy Spirit, as well as with the dispensation concerning us, with resurrection and judgment, and who follows the rules of the church is not in schism. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: APPEAL IN CHRIST’S NAME.
entries.body: Paul was right to add the name of Christ here, because that is what the Corinthians were really rejecting. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 167

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: UNITED IN THINKING.
entries.body: Paul prays that the Corinthians will all think one thing, namely, that those who have been born again are children of God. He wants them to be perfectly united in the teaching which he had given to them. He challenges them to think this way and to defend his teaching. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: UNITED IN JUDGMENT.
entries.body: It is possible to agree on a form of words but still harbor dissent, which is why Paul speaks the way he does here. It is also possible to share the same opinion with someone but not the same feelings. For example, it is possible to be united in faith without being united in love. This is why Paul says that we must be united both in mind and in judgment. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 3.2

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: WHETHER CHLOE IS A PLACE.
entries.body: Some people think that Chloe’s people are those who remain faithful and bear fruit in the faith of Christ. Others think that Chloe is a place, as if one were to say Antioch’s people, for example. But others think that she was a woman devoted to God, in whose company there were many faithful worshipers. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: PAUL’S SOURCE NOT PERSONALLY IDENTIFIED.
entries.body: Paul is careful to mention who his informers are without singling out one particular person. This gives his criticisms plausibility without allowing the Corinthians to direct their feelings toward any one person. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 3.3

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: WHETHER CHLOE IS A FAMILY.
entries.body: Perhaps there was a family at Corinth called Chloe, but Paul does not give any details so as not to reveal their identity and start more quarreling. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 168

entries.author: St. Clement of Rome
entries.title: CORINTH HAD A HISTORY OF PARTISANSHIP.
entries.body: Inspired by God, Paul wrote to you concerning himself and Cephas and Apollos, because even then you were given to faction. But that factiousness involved you in less guilt, because then you were partisans of highly reputed apostles and of those commended by them. {fn:0}

footnotes.text: FC 1:46. Clement of Rome was writing in the A.D. 90s to the church at Corinth, recalling the tendency to partisanship “even then,” in the times of Paul.

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: ALL GOOD TEACHERS.
entries.body: Paul exposes their error without mentioning the names of the people responsible. The men whom he names here were all good teachers, but by alluding to them in this way he is really getting at the false apostles. For if the Corinthians were not to boast of their devotion to any of these men, how much more would this be true in the case of false teachers, whose corrupt doctrine he refers to next? {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: IS CHRIST THE HEAD OF A FACTION?
entries.body: The quarreling at Corinth was not over trivial matters but over something fundamental. Even those who said they were of Christ were at fault, because they were implicitly denying this to others and making Christ the head of a faction rather than the head of the whole church. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 3.5

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: A RIDICULOUS CONFLICT.
entries.body: In reality the Corinthians called themselves after other teachers, but Paul uses his own name and that of Apollos and Peter in order to make his point. By adding the name of Christ to the rest, he showed them how ridiculous the whole conflict was. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 168

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: HOW CHRIST BECOMES DIVIDED.
entries.body:
By believing different things about Christ, the people have divided him. One person thought that Christ was a mere man, another that he was only God. One says that he was foretold by the prophets, while another denies it.

Paul starts with himself, so that nobody will think that he is disparaging the status of others. If Christ died for us, how can we attribute his grace and blessing to men, thereby doing him a grave injustice? {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE QUESTION RHETORICAL.
entries.body: Whenever Paul uses rhetorical questions, as he does here, he implies that the whole argument is absurd. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 3.5

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: CLAIMING BAPTISM IN THE WRONG NAMES.
entries.body: These Corinthians were like the Novatianists and the Donatists of today, who claim baptism for themselves and do not recognize anybody else’s. Those who are so baptized glory in the names of Novatian and Donatus, having been deprived of the name of Christ. Crispus and Gaius are called as witnesses, because although they were baptized by Paul, they never suggested that he should be given any glory because of it. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE NAME INVOKED IN BAPTISM.
entries.body: The greatness of baptism does not lie in the baptizer but in the one whose name is invoked in the baptism. Furthermore, although baptism is important and even necessary in order to obtain the kingdom, still it is much less than preaching the gospel. A person of no singular excellence can baptize, but only the truly gifted can preach the gospel. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 3.6

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: PAUL NOT SEEKING GLORY.
entries.body: Paul downplays his own role in order to show that he was not seeking honor or glory for himself. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 3.6

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: WHY FISHERS WERE CHOSEN TO PREACH.
entries.body: Because it is a greater thing to preach the gospel than to baptize, Paul says that he was sent to do the former, not the latter. Not everyone who baptizes is competent to preach the gospel, for the words used at baptism are an established formula. When Cornelius became a believer, the apostle Peter gave orders that he should be baptized along with his household, but he did not bother to do it himself when he had his assistants standing by.{fn:0} It was because Christian preaching does not need elaborate refinement of verbal expression that fishers, who were uneducated, were chosen to preach the gospel.{fn:1} In that way the truth of the message would be its own recommendation, and it would not depend on the cleverness or ingenuity of human wisdom. The false apostles were doing just that, and moreover they were omitting the things which the world does not believe, like the virgin birth of Christ and his resurrection from the dead. {fn:2}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: PAUL’S APOSTOLIC OFFICE TOOK PRECEDENCE.
entries.body: Paul did not usurp the functions of the lower offices when he was able to exercise the higher ones. It would be as if nowadays a bishop or even a priest were to do the work of a deacon. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 1

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE CROSS STANDS CONTRARY TO HUMAN WISDOM.
entries.body: If human wisdom is at war with the cross and fights against the gospel, it is not right to boast about it. Rather, we should recoil in shame. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 3.7

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: PREACHING IS A GIFT.
entries.body: Anyone can baptize if he is a priest, but preaching is a gift given to few, and it must not be confused with mere eloquence, which is purely superficial. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 169

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: A DIVINE DEFENSE AGAINST THE DEVIL.
entries.body: Who was capable of destroying the plague of ignorance, darkness and destruction? Not a prophet, nor an apostle, nor any other righteous man. Rather there had to be a divine power coming down from heaven, capable of dying on behalf of us all, so that by his death there might be a defense against the devil. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE MADNESS OF REJECTING GOOD MEDICINE.
entries.body: The power of the cross is not recognized by those who are perishing, because they are out of their minds and act like madmen, complaining and rejecting the medicines which bring salvation. {fn:0} THWARTING THE CLEVERNESS OF THE CLEVER.{fn:0} AMBROSIASTER: By doing this, God shows that actions speak louder than words. {fn:1}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: FOOD FOR DECEPTION.
entries.body: Paul speaks about the wisdom of this world and not merely the eloquence, for God has given it also. It was God who divided the languages and gave each one its own character. It was he who gave the Greek language its splendor. But those who abuse these gifts have prepared food for deception and have preached false tales. What Paul objects to is not their eloquence as such but the false teaching which lies behind it. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 170-71

entries.author: St. Ignatius of Antioch
entries.title: THE CROSS A STUMBLING BLOCK.
entries.body: My spirit has become an offscouring of the cross, which is a stumbling block to those who are unbelievers, but to us it is salvation and life eternal. Where is the wise man? Where is the debater? Where is the boasting of those who are called prudent? {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE HUMBLING OF GREEK WISDOM AND JEWISH LAW.
entries.body: Here Paul attacks the Jews as much as the Gentiles, because their scribes and doctors of the law think that it is foolish to believe that God has a Son. Gentiles also laugh at this, but the Jews’ unbelief is based on the fact that the matter is not openly stated in the law, whereas the Gentiles think it is silly because the reasoning of the world does not accept it, claiming that nothing can be made without sexual union. The debater of this age is a man who thinks that the world is governed by the conjunction of the stars and that births and deaths are brought about by the twelve signs of the zodiac. Is there anything more foolish than the belief that the Creator does not care about the world he has made? What would be the point of making it in that case? It is because they see some people enjoying life and others not, because they see the righteous suffering while the wicked boast, that they have come to believe that God does not care. But to say this is to say that God is malevolent and unjust. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Hilary of Poitiers
entries.title: THE WISE AND THE PRUDENT.
entries.body: In this matter the wise and the prudent are silent, for they have rejected the wisdom of God. {fn:0}

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.body: The wise man is the Greek, the scribe is the Jew. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: THE TRUE WISDOM.
entries.body: The wisdom of this world and the wisdom of God are not the same thing. God’s wisdom is the true one, without any additives to corrupt it. The world’s wisdom is foolish, even though the simplicity of God’s wisdom makes those who have it appear foolish in the eyes of the world. Believers have received this divine wisdom and thus in this world appear to be fools. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Clement of Alexandria
entries.title: GOD’S WISDOM CONVEYED THROUGH HIS SON.
entries.body: Paul says that the wisdom of God is teaching in conformity with the Lord, which will show that true philosophy is conveyed through the Son. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Athanasius of Alexandria
entries.title: GOD’S WISDOM HAS BECOME INCARNATE.
entries.body: Given that men had rejected the contemplation of God and were looking for him in nature and in the material world, making gods for themselves out of mortal men and demons, the loving and general Savior of all, the Word of God, took to himself a body and walked about like a man, in order to meet the senses halfway, so that those who think that God is corporeal might perceive the truth by observing what the Lord accomplishes in his body, and through him recognize the Father. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: PREACHING THAT SEEMS FOOLISH.
entries.body: The world has not recognized God but has attributed divine majesty to his creatures and to the elemental powers of the universe, thinking that visible things ought to be worshiped.{fn:0} God has therefore chosen a form of preaching which will seem foolish to such people. Those who reject what the apostles preach will be condemned, while believers are being saved. {fn:1}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: HUMAN WISDOM DOES NOT CONVERT.
entries.body: To believe in the one who was crucified and buried and to be fully convinced that he rose again does not need more reasoning but faith alone. The apostles themselves were converted not by wisdom but by faith. Once they had that, they surpassed the heathen wise men in both wisdom and intellectual depth. . . . Plato was cast out not by another philosopher of more skill but by unlearned fishers. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 4.4

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: THE FOLLY OF THE CROSS DISTINGUISHED FROM TWO OTHER KINDS OF WISDOM.
entries.body: Paul enumerates two or even three different kinds of wisdom here. First there is what the world calls folly, wisdom greater than the others. Then there is the wisdom given to human beings by which we reason and act, by which we develop and invent things and by which we can know God. Finally, there is a third kind of wisdom, which is found in the contemplation of the creation. The wisdom which is folly to the world is given to us by the Savior, so that people who know God by natural wisdom and who are led to him by contemplating the created order may attain the salvation which neither of these kinds of wisdom can provide and be delivered from error. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 171

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: JEWS DEMAND SIGNS, GREEKS SEEK ARGUMENTS.
entries.body: The Jews seek signs because they do not reject the possibility that things like this can happen. What they want to know is whether it has actually occurred, like Aaron’s rod, which sprouted and bore fruit,{fn:0} and Jonah who spent three days and nights in the belly of the whale before being spewed out alive.{fn:1} But the Greeks seek wisdom, refusing to believe anything which does not accord with human reason. {fn:2}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: BOTH EVIDENCE DISBELIEF.
entries.body: The Jews want signs, because that is what the prophets gave them, but even then they do not believe. The Greeks, on the other hand, want clever academic arguments. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 1

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: EMPOWERMENT THROUGH THE CRUCIFIED.
entries.body: What has empowered us is belief in Christ crucified. To the extent that we are lacking something in our faith, then we are missing out on what the power of God has to offer us. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: A STUMBLING BLOCK AND FOLLY.
entries.body: It is a stumbling block to the Jews when they hear Christ calling himself the Son of God yet not observing the sabbath. It is foolishness to the Gentiles because they hear things like the virgin birth and the resurrection being preached but regard them as irrational. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE WISDOM OF THE FATHER KNOWN THROUGH THE SON.
entries.body: When Jews believe in Christ, they understand that he is the power of God. When Greeks believe in him, they understand that he is the wisdom of God. He is God’s power because the Father does everything through him. He is God’s wisdom because God is known through him. It would not be possible for God to be known through anyone who was not from him in the first place. No one has seen the Father except the Son and whomever the Son has chosen to reveal him to. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE OFFENSE AND THE CALL.
entries.body: The gospel produces the exact opposite of what people want and expect, but it is that very fact which persuades them to accept it in the end. The apostles won their case, not simply without a sign, but by something which appeared to go against all the known signs. The cross seems to be a cause of offense, but far from simply offending, it attracts and calls believers to itself. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 4.5

entries.author: St. Clement of Alexandria
entries.title: GOD’S POWER IS SUFFICIENT.
entries.body: The Savior’s teaching is sufficient without additional help, for it is the power and wisdom of God. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Gregory of Nyssa
entries.title: GOD’S WISDOM IS INTERWOVEN WITH GOD’S POWER.
entries.body: Through this description of Christ we derive notions of the divine which make the name an object of reverence for us. Since all creation, both perceptible and imperceptible, came into being through him and is united with him, wisdom is necessarily interwoven with power in connection with the very definition of Christ, the maker of all things. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: THE CROSS AS PATTERN.
entries.body: Note that Paul does not say that the divinity of Christ is God’s power and wisdom, but rather the pattern of the cross. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 1

entries.author: Theodore of Mopsuestia
entries.title: PREACHING THE CROSS.
entries.body: The power and wisdom of God is not the divinity of Christ as such but the preaching of the cross. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: GOD’S WISDOM CONFOUNDS HUMAN REASONING.
entries.body: When Paul speaks of the foolishness of God, he is not implying that God is foolish. Rather he is saying that since God’s way of reasoning is in accord with things of the spirit, it confounds the reasoning of this world. It is wiser than human reasoning, because spiritual things are wiser than carnal ones. Spiritual things do not exist through carnal ones, but the other way around. Therefore carnal things are understandable in relation to spiritual ones. Similarly, what belongs to heaven is stronger than what belongs to earth. So what seems like the weakness of God is not really weak at all. Christ appeared to be defeated when he was killed, but he emerged as the victor and turned the reproof back on his persecutors. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: CHRIST’S WORLD MISSION CARRIED OUT BY THE UNLEARNED TO EVIDENCE GOD’S POWER.
entries.body: The folly and the wisdom spoken of here are more apparent than real. For the philosophers could not achieve what a few unlearned men accomplished, namely, the conversion of the whole world. The philosophers spoke about trivialities and convinced only a few. The apostles spoke about God, righteousness and judgment and converted a great many. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 4.6

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE GOSPEL TRUTH.
entries.body: There is no way that this gospel truth could have been made acceptable to some philosophers and debaters. They follow a way of life that does not stand in the truth but is only an imitation of it. They deceived themselves and others. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: WORLDLY STANDARDS.
entries.body: The man who is wise according to the standards of this world is really very foolish, because he will not cast away his corrupt teaching. A little learning is a dangerous thing, because it makes those who have it unwilling to learn more. The unlearned are more open to conviction, because they are not so foolish as to think that they are wise. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 5.2

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: NOT MANY WERE POWERFUL.
entries.body: Paul did not say that there was nobody like this in the church, only that there were not many. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 173

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: WEAK THINGS A SOURCE OF POWER.
entries.body: The two most foolish things of the world are in particular the virgin birth of Christ and his resurrection from the dead. The wise are confounded because they see that what a few of them deny, the many profess to be true. There is no doubt that the opinions of the many faithful take precedence over those of a small number. Likewise, those who are mighty in this world can easily see the so-called weak things of Christ overturning demons and performing miracles. To the world the injuries and sufferings of the Savior are weak things, because the world does not understand that they have become the source of power through Christ who submitted to suffering in order to overcome death. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: GETTING THE BETTER OF THE PHILOSOPHERS.
entries.body: In human terms, it was not possible for fishers to get the better of philosophers, but that is what happened by the power of God’s grace. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 5.5

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: GOD CHOSE WHAT IS LOW.
entries.body: God chose ignoble and contemptible things to exalt. It is not that they are really ignoble and contemptible; this is how the world sees them. By believing in Christ they have overturned worldly reasoning. God did this in order to destroy things which are really ignoble and contemptible, because those who judged were more deserving of judgment and condemnation. Their teaching was asserted in words but not demonstrated in power, and so it was destroyed. Our teaching is proved true not only by words but by power as well. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodore of Mopsuestia
entries.title: THINGS THAT ARE NOT.
entries.body: Paul uses the expression things that are not differently here from the way he uses it in his epistle to the Romans. Here it means simply that which is vile and contemptible, as opposed to things that are, which are beautiful, powerful and respected. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: UNDER THE JUDGMENT OF GOD.
entries.body: Under the judgment of God the wisdom of the flesh can only blush at its miscalculations. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: HUMBLING THOSE IN HIGH PLACES.
entries.body: God did not just choose the unlearned, but also the needy, the contemptible and the obscure, in order to humble those in high places. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 5.3

entries.author: Theodore of Mopsuestia
entries.title: THE HARM OF BOASTING.
entries.body: Boasting, even if it is of good works, harms the soul of the boaster. Anyone who boasts of worldly achievements is highly worldly himself. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: ATTACKING PRIDE.
entries.body: Paul’s intention is perfectly clear—to accost the pride of man, that no one should take glory in human works and that no one should glory in himself. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: THE TEMPTATION OF THE ELOQUENT.
entries.body: If Paul had chosen only the most eloquent and gifted people as preachers they would have gloried in their own abilities and been damned for it, whether they preached the truth or not. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 173

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: GOD MADE CHRIST OUR SANCTIFICATION.
entries.body: Christ was made our sanctification, not so that he might change what he was but that he might sanctify us in the flesh. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: OUR REDEMPTION.
entries.body: Christ did what he did in order to strengthen believers, for no one can redeem something which did not originally belong to him. Therefore, whether it is because we have been redeemed, or because we have been sanctified (i.e., purged from the works of the flesh and the filthiness of idols), or because we have been justified (for it is just to worship only the Creator and spurn everything else) or because we are wise, having learned that worldly people are unwise—all this is a gift of God through Christ. But this is our redemption—when the devil desires it, Christ offers himself to the devil so that he may cancel sin and rescue the devil’s captives. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Gregory of Nyssa
entries.title: OUR RANSOM.
entries.body: We are taught by the knowledge that Christ is redemption, because he gave himself as an atonement on our behalf, that when he bestowed immortality on us as our own possession, he ransomed us from death with his own life. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: OUR SALVATION.
entries.body: God did not just make us wise, righteous and holy in Christ. He gave us Christ so that we should never need anything else for our salvation. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 5.4

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: BOAST OF THE LORD.
entries.body: What Jeremiah [as quoted here] says is commendable, because the person who glories in the Lord will not be confounded. {fn:0}
